name: Centimanes
description: The Centimanes, also known as the Hecatoncheires (meaning 'hundred-handed ones'), were monstrous giants in Greek mythology, children of Uranus (Heaven) and Gaia (Earth). There were three of them: Cottus, Briareus (also called Aegaeon), and Gyges. Each Centimanes possessed immense strength and was characterized by having one hundred hands and fifty heads. Their hideous appearance and overwhelming power initially terrified their father, Uranus, who imprisoned them in Tartarus along with the Cyclopes. They remained imprisoned until freed by Zeus during the Titanomachy. Their unparalleled might proved crucial in the Olympian gods' victory against the Titans. After the war, Briareus was granted a sea palace and became a protector of Poseidon. The Centimanes represent primal, untamed power and the raw force of nature. Their hundred hands symbolize their ability to accomplish a great deal and their fifty heads suggest immense awareness and vigilance. They are a testament to the chaotic and powerful forces that existed before the establishment of the Olympian order.
subtitle: Hundred-Handed Giants of Greek Myth
appearance: The Centimanes were colossal giants, each possessing fifty heads and one hundred arms. Their physical form was described as grotesque and terrifying, inspiring fear even in the gods. Their multiple heads allowed for heightened awareness and their numerous hands granted them incredible strength and dexterity. They were said to be larger and more powerful than the Titans themselves.
habitat: Originally imprisoned in Tartarus, later residing near or in the sea, particularly Briareus after being freed by Zeus.
behavior: The Centimanes were initially perceived as chaotic and destructive, leading to their imprisonment. However, after being freed by Zeus, they demonstrated loyalty and proved to be invaluable allies in the Titanomachy. They are characterized by their immense strength and unwavering commitment to those they support. While formidable in battle, they are not depicted as inherently malevolent.

keyMyths.title: Imprisonment in Tartarus
keyMyths.description: Uranus, fearful of the Centimanes' power, imprisoned them in Tartarus along with the Cyclopes. This act demonstrates the primal fear of unchecked power and the desire to control chaotic forces.

keyMyths.title: The Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: Zeus freed the Centimanes from Tartarus to aid the Olympians in their battle against the Titans. Their immense strength proved decisive in securing victory for the Olympian gods.

keyMyths.title: Briareus and Thetis
keyMyths.description: When the other Olympian gods plotted to bind Zeus, Thetis called upon Briareus to protect him. Briareus' presence alone deterred the other gods from carrying out their plan, showcasing his overwhelming power.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A foundational text in Greek mythology that describes the origins of the gods and the Centimanes.
sources.text: A foundational text in Greek mythology that describes the origins of the gods and the Centimanes.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive compendium of Greek myths and legends, providing details on the Centimanes' role in the Titanomachy.
